title: Emergency Guide: Dog with Blue Gums and Gasping for Air
meta_desc: Blue gums and gasping for air in dogs is a critical emergency. Learn why this happens and why you must seek immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Blue or purple gums and gasping for air indicate a life-threatening lack of oxygen. Seek immediate emergency veterinary care now, as your dog requires professional oxygen therapy to survive.

Why are my dog's gums blue and why are they gasping for air?

When a dog has blue, purple, or grayish gums, it is a clinical condition called cyanosis. This occurs because there is not enough oxygen in the bloodstream. Gasping for air, also known as dyspnea, is a sign that your dog is struggling to breathe. Together, these symptoms indicate that your dog's vital organs are being deprived of life-sustaining oxygen due to a failure in the respiratory or cardiovascular system.

Should I take my dog to an emergency vet for blue gums and breathing issues?

  • Urgency Level: High (Critical). This is a life-threatening emergency.
  • Do not wait for an appointment or monitor the situation at home.
  • Your dog requires immediate oxygen therapy and professional intervention.
  • Contact your nearest 24-hour emergency veterinary hospital immediately to let them know you are arriving with a patient in respiratory distress.

How can taking a photo or video of my dog help the emergency veterinary team?

  • If a passenger can safely take a photo of your dog's gums or a brief video of their breathing while you drive, it can be incredibly helpful for the triage team.
  • Sending this media ahead or showing it upon arrival allows the staff to instantly assess the severity of the hypoxia.
  • Visual evidence helps the team prepare the oxygen cage, intubation tools, or emergency medications before you even enter the building.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs presenting with blue gums (cyanosis) and gasping for air, immediate assessment of the airway and breathing is critical. Cyanosis, labored open-mouth breathing, and changes in respiratory pattern indicate significant pulmonary dysfunction and impending pulmonary arrest. Possible causes of large airway obstruction include foreign bodies, edema, laryngeal paralysis or paresis, tracheal collapse, elongated soft palate, aspiration of stomach contents, neoplasia, and pharyngeal hematomas. Auscultation may reveal referred airway sounds, stridor, or wheezes. Unconscious, apneic animals require immediate tracheal intubation, and any airway obstruction must be immediately relieved. Compromised breathing manifests as increased respiratory rate and effort, postural changes (orthopnea), and can be further evaluated via careful observation of the breathing pattern and auscultation of the thorax to determine the location of the pathology.

Chapter: Emergency, Anesthesiology, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1662)

faqs.question: What are common causes of blue gums in dogs?
faqs.answer: Potential causes include congestive heart failure, pneumonia, choking on a foreign object, allergic reactions (anaphylaxis), or lung collapse.

faqs.question: Can I perform CPR on my dog?
faqs.answer: CPR should only be performed if the dog has stopped breathing and has no pulse. If they are still gasping, focus entirely on getting them to a vet immediately.

faqs.question: Should I try to open my dog's mouth to look for a blockage?
faqs.answer: Only attempt this if you are certain they are choking and you can see the object clearly. Be extremely careful, as a distressed dog may bite reflexively.
